title: Great Books #9:  Dante (Re-Upload with Audio Fixed)
summary:
In this Wednesday, April 8, 2026 lecture to his Beijing high school students, Professor Jiang introduces Dante's Divine Comedy.  

Notes and References:
1.  Dante:  https://digitaldante.columbia.edu/dante/divine-comedy/

The Divine Comedy is the greatest literary masterpiece in human history. When Dante wrote it in about 1300, he called it LaMada. Okay. And the reason why is that at this time in history, epic poetry was considered high, okay, or tragic. And it was written usually in Latin.

But for Dante, what he really believed is that epic poetry should be democratic. It should be accessible to the people. That's why he called it laadia low. Okay. Which meant comedy and so he wrote it in Tuskin which is the language of Florence where he lived. Um Latin was the language of the elite in Europe. He wanted to write in Tuskin so that ordinary people around him could

access it. And as a result, Dante is a great believer in democracy. So, uh, Homer, Dante and Shakespeare were in their own respective languages the greatest poets because first and foremost they believe that poetry should be accessed by the people. They believed in um a democratic spirit to

poetry and that made them distinct from the people of their time. Lamadia is response then to the Iniad by Virgil. At this time in history, for the past 1,000 years, the INAD was a dominant dominant literature in Europe and it was the foundation for the Catholic Church. Okay. So remember when we read the iniad the end is fundamentally about duty

uh piety um and it just spokes and it contrasted love. Okay. And this became the dominant idea in the Catholic Church where you are not allowed to access God. You're not allowed to believe in love. The second is paradox. All right. So imagine laadia as not just epic poetry but as a very complex mathematical puzzle that you must unravel through time. Okay.
